name: WebAssembly Micro Runtime (WAMR)
summary: Bytecode Alliance embedded-targeted Wasm runtime: C, kilobyte-budget, multi-mode execution (interpreter / fast interpreter / AOT / JIT). Supports Wasmtime-class semantics on MCU-class hardware. Multi-mode design lets the same module run differently across deployment contexts.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: WebAssembly Micro Runtime (WAMR), maintained by the Bytecode Alliance, is the embedded-targeted Wasm runtime. Written in C with a kilobyte-budget footprint, it targets MCU-class and small-Linux deployments where the multi-megabyte runtimes (Wasmtime, Wasmer, WAVM) are too heavy.

sections.title: Multi-mode execution
sections.content:
WAMR supports multiple execution modes that can be selected per deployment:

- **Classic interpreter** — smallest footprint, lowest performance.
- **Fast interpreter** — somewhat faster via decoded bytecode pre-processing, slightly larger footprint.
- **AOT (ahead-of-time)** — compile to native at build time, ship the native code; near-native performance, no runtime compilation.
- **JIT** — runtime LLVM compilation when available; useful for dynamic deployment.

The multi-mode design lets the same Wasm module run on a Cortex-M MCU (interpreter mode, kilobytes of RAM) and on a Linux server (AOT or JIT mode, native performance) with no source change.

sections.title: Design implication
sections.content: The Wasm runtime spectrum spans from QuickJS-class interpreter-only embedded engines (kilobyte footprint, slow) through WAMR (kilobyte to megabyte footprint, choose-your-mode) up to LLVM-only WAVM (megabyte footprint, peak performance). For language designers compiling to Wasm, WAMR provides the broadest deployment surface — the same artifact runs across the spectrum without recompilation.
